title: Sewn-boards binding
subtitle: Sewing signatures directly onto wooden or thick board spines without a separate case
excerpt: In a sewn-boards binding, the cover boards are part of the sewing structure itself. The first and last signatures sew directly to the boards, and the spine is left exposed or lined but not covered. The result is strong, lies flat, and shows the sewing openly.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on forwarding and attachment. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. The sewn-boards structure is documented in Cockerell as a variant of long-stitch sewing used in the medieval period before case binding became standard.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cord, tape, or recessed groove over or through which the sewing thread loops as it passes from one signature to the next, creating a structural link between signatures.
The linking stitch worked at the head and tail of each signature, or around a sewing support, that ties successive signatures together into a cohesive text block.
The sewn group of signatures before covers are attached.

projectSchedule.title: Prepare signatures and boards
projectSchedule.body: Fold and fold the signatures, pierce the sewing stations, and cut and bevel the boards. Allow the PVA on the boards to dry fully before sewing if you have pre-treated them.

projectSchedule.title: Sew the binding
projectSchedule.body: Sew signatures through the boards using the sewing supports. This is the main working session.

projectSchedule.title: Finish and press
projectSchedule.body: Consolidate the spine, trim if needed, and press overnight.

content.text: In conventional case binding, the covers are made separately and glued onto a finished
text block
. In a sewn-boards binding, the front and back boards are present from the first stitch. The outer signature sews through holes in the boards as well as through its own sewing stations, and the
sewing supports
lace through or around the boards as well as the text block. The book's structural integrity comes from the thread, not from glue; a well-sewn binding can outlast several rebinds.
What you need

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones for cutting board. Change the craft-knife blade frequently when scoring binders board: the dense material dulls a blade within two or three cuts.

content.text: Prepare the signatures

Fold the pages into signatures of four or five sheets each (80 to 100 gsm cartridge, long-grain with the grain parallel to the fold). The text block needs at least six signatures for the sewn-boards structure to read clearly. Pierce three sewing stations per signature: head, tail, and centre.
Prepare the boards

Cut two boards from 3 mm binders board, each 3 mm taller than the text block (1.5 mm at head and tail) and 3 mm wider at the foredge. The spine edge of each board sits flush with the text block's spine.
Pierce three holes through the spine edge of each board, aligned with the sewing stations: 10 mm from the head, 10 mm from the tail, and centred.
Cut a shallow groove on the outer board face between each pair of holes. Use the craft knife and ruler. The groove holds the thread and keeps it level with the board surface.
Sewing
Thread the needle with a length of waxed linen thread roughly six times the text block spine height. Begin by passing the thread through the head hole of the front board, from outside to inside.
Sew through the first signature in the standard long-stitch sequence: enter at head, exit at centre, re-enter at centre, exit at tail. Each exit should link to the board hole or the previous
link stitch
before moving to the next signature.
Continue sewing through each successive signature, linking to the previous signature's thread at the head, centre, and tail stations. Pull the thread firm but not tight enough to cut into the paper.
On the final signature, pass through the back board holes in the same sequence as the front board, anchoring the back board to the text block. The boards are now structurally integral to the sewing.
Tie off the thread end at the last hole with a square knot and tuck the tail beneath the outermost thread run.
Finishing

Apply a thin coat of acid-free PVA to the spine to consolidate the thread runs, wiping off the excess with a damp cloth. Press the book in the book press overnight. The boards may be left plain, painted, or covered in a single layer of book cloth applied after the binding is fully dry.

items.symptom: The boards pivot and do not open squarely
items.cause: The spine-edge holes in the boards are too close together, leaving no hinge point.
items.fix: Ensure the board holes align precisely with the sewing stations. The board hinge is at the inner face of the spine-edge groove; the groove must be the correct depth.

items.symptom: Thread shows prominently on the board outer face
items.cause: The groove on the board face is too shallow.
items.fix: Deepen the groove with repeated craft-knife passes until the thread sits flush when pulled taut.
